epigraph.text:
The race is not to the swift or the battle to the 
strong... but time and chance happen to them all.

content.text:
Knowing the center of a data set is helpful, but it doesn't tell the 
whole story. Are the values clustered tightly together, or spread far 
apart? Are there unusual values that stand out? In this section, we 
explore the **range** and learn to identify **outliers**—
values that don't fit the pattern.

The **range** measures how spread out a data set is by finding the 
difference between the largest and smallest values.

content.title: Range
content.content:
The **range** of a data set is the difference between 
the maximum and minimum values:
\[
\text{Range} = \text{Maximum} - \text{Minimum}
\]

content.title: Finding the range
content.problem:
Find the range of each data set:

1. Test scores: 72, 85, 88, 91, 95

2. Daily temperatures: 68°F, 75°F, 71°F, 82°F, 64°F
content.solution:
1. Maximum = 95, Minimum = 72
 
 Range = $95 - 72 = \textbf{23}$ points

2. Maximum = 82°F, Minimum = 64°F
 
 Range = $82 - 64 = \textbf{18}$°F

content.title: Comparing ranges
content.problem:
Two classes took the same test:

- Class A scores: 70, 75, 80, 85, 90

- Class B scores: 60, 72, 80, 88, 100
Compare the ranges and what they tell us about each class.
content.solution:
Class A: Range = $90 - 70 = 20$ points

Class B: Range = $100 - 60 = 40$ points

Both classes have the same mean (80), but Class B has a much larger 
range. This means Class B's scores are more spread out—there's greater 
variability in student performance. Class A's scores are more consistent.

content.text:
An **outlier** is a value that is significantly different from 
most other values in the data set. Outliers can occur because of 
measurement errors, unusual circumstances, or genuine variation.

content.title: Outlier
content.content:
An **outlier** is a data value that is much larger 
or much smaller than most other values in the data set.

content.title: Identifying outliers
content.solution:
Most values are between 12 and 16. The value **42** is far larger 
than the others—it's an outlier.

This could be a data entry error (perhaps 14 was typed as 42) or a 
genuine unusual value. We should investigate before deciding what to do.

content.text:
There is no single rule for identifying outliers, but a common approach 
uses the "1.5 $\times$ IQR rule" (which you'll learn in later courses). 
For now, use your judgment: if a value seems far removed from the 
others, it's likely an outlier.
How Outliers Affect the Mean and Median

Outliers have a dramatic effect on the mean but little effect on the 
median. This is why the median is often preferred when outliers are 
present.

content.title: Effect of an outlier on mean vs.\ median
content.problem:
Consider two data sets:

- Set A: 10, 12, 14, 16, 18

- Set B: 10, 12, 14, 16, 100 (18 replaced by 100)
Calculate the mean and median for each set.
content.solution:
**Set A:**
$$
\text{Mean} &= \frac{10 + 12 + 14 + 16 + 18}{5} = \frac{70}{5} = 14 \\
\text{Median} &= 14 \quad \text{(middle value)}
$$

**Set B:**
$$
\text{Mean} &= \frac{10 + 12 + 14 + 16 + 100}{5} = \frac{152}{5} = 30.4 \\
\text{Median} &= 14 \quad \text{(middle value---unchanged!)}
$$

The outlier (100) increased the mean from 14 to 30.4—more than 
doubling it! But the median stayed at 14. This shows why the median 
is more **resistant** to outliers.

content.title: Dealing with Outliers
content.content:
When you encounter an outlier:

1. **Investigate.** Is it a data entry error? A measurement 
 mistake? Or a genuine unusual value?

2. **If it's an error,** correct it or remove it.

3. **If it's genuine,** consider using the median instead of 
 the mean to describe the center.

4. **Report it.** Always mention outliers when describing 
 your data—don't hide them!

content.title: When outliers matter
content.problem:
A company reports that its employees earn an "average" of \$85,000. 
The data: \$32,000; \$38,000; \$35,000; \$41,000; \$280,000 (the CEO).
content.solution:
Mean = $\frac{32,000 + 38,000 + 35,000 + 41,000 + 280,000}{5} = 
\frac{426,000}{5} = \$85,200$

Median: In order: 32,000; 35,000; 38,000; 41,000; 280,000. 
The median is \$38,000.

The \$85,000 ``average'' is technically correct, but misleading. Four 
of five employees earn less than half that amount! A more honest 
description would report the median (\$38,000) or note that the mean 
is pulled up by one very high salary.

content.text:
Outliers have a major effect on the range because the range depends 
only on the maximum and minimum values.

content.title: Outliers and range
content.problem:
Consider the ages at a birthday party:

- Without outlier: 7, 8, 8, 9, 9, 10. Range = $10 - 7 = 3$

- With outlier: 7, 8, 8, 9, 9, 10, 42 (a parent's age). 
 Range = $42 - 7 = 35$

Adding one adult changes the range from 3 years to 35 years—a huge 
increase that makes the range less useful for describing typical 
variation among the children.

content.title: The Challenger Disaster
content.content:
On January 28, 1986, the Space Shuttle Challenger exploded shortly 
after launch, killing all seven crew members. Engineers had warned that 
the O-ring seals might fail in cold weather. But when they presented 
data showing O-ring problems, they excluded data from successful 
launches—only showing failures. Had all data been included, the 
pattern would have been clearer. This tragedy illustrates why we must 
never cherry-pick data or hide outliers to support a conclusion.

marginNotes.text:
In the beginning God created the heavens 
and the earth.

The prudent see danger and take refuge, 
but the simple keep going and pay the penalty.

marginNotes.author: Florence Nightingale
marginNotes.text:
To understand God's thoughts, we must 
study statistics, for these are the measure of His purpose.
